tldr: Simon, roughly 60 of your 156 live ads are running the same 'Built To Last. Cut In Britain.' headline with the exact same Phil Vickery founder story word-for-word. Meta treats these as basically one ad, so most of them get almost no views even though you paid to make them.

benchmark.context: The typical menswear brand can leave an ad running about three weeks before it wears out. At your ad volume, fresh ads should be replacing tired ones every 10 days or so. You're shipping new ads often enough, but they're mostly copies of the same one.

ads.headline: Built To Last. Cut In Britain.
ads.body: Started on a rugby pitch. Built for everyone off it. Phil Vickery founded Raging Bull in 2003 with a simple idea: clothing that fits properly, lasts longer, and doesn't compromise on quality.  Over 20 years later, that's still the whole point.
drivers: This exact headline and Phil Vickery paragraph is running word-for-word on over 40 of your live ads right now.
Meta sees these near-identical ads as basically one ad, so it quietly picks one and stops showing the rest.
It's been live 36 days, well past the 10 day mark where your best ads should be getting a rest.

ads.headline: Built To Last. Cut In Britain.
ads.body: Started on a rugby pitch. Built for everyone off it. Phil Vickery founded Raging Bull in 2003 with a simple idea: clothing that fits properly, lasts longer, and doesn't compromise on quality.  Over 20 years later, that's still the whole point.
drivers: Same founder story, same headline, sent to the flash sale page - you have four other ads doing the exact same thing.
36 days old, so it's been fighting its own copies for over a month while you paid for every day.
The words never once mention what the shopper actually sees when they land, so people bounce.

ads.headline: Built To Last. Cut In Britain.
ads.body: Started on a rugby pitch. Built for everyone off it. Phil Vickery founded Raging Bull in 2003 with a simple idea: clothing that fits properly, lasts longer, and doesn't compromise on quality.  Over 20 years later, that's still the whole point.
drivers: This is the founder story ad pointed at the '2 for £40 tees' page, but the ad never mentions tees or the deal.
Someone clicking expects the story to continue on the page they land on. Instead they see a price offer they weren't sold.
36 days of running with no offer in the words means the shoppers who care about the deal never click.

ads.headline: Built To Last. Cut In Britain.
ads.body: Started on a rugby pitch. Built for everyone off it. Phil Vickery founded Raging Bull in 2003 with a simple idea: clothing that fits properly, lasts longer, and doesn't compromise on quality.  Over 20 years later, that's still the whole point.
drivers: 36 days on the same founder story with no picture of a product, so people can't see what they'd get.
Meta groups this with all your other founder-story ads and only shows one of them to any given person.
The ad points to the general t-shirt page, so the shopper has to hunt for anything they might actually buy.

ads.headline: Built To Last. Cut In Britain.
ads.body: Summer's not over, but the prices just dropped. Up to 50% off swim shorts, shirts, polos and tees. Last of the season, while sizes last. Headline: Up to 50% Off. End of Season Sale.
drivers: The words 'Headline: Up to 50% Off. End of Season Sale.' were left in the body by mistake - that's a note from your workflow that made it live.
You have seven copies of this exact sale ad running side by side. Meta shows one and hides the rest.
The main headline says 'Built To Last' but the body says '50% off' - the message fights itself.

ads_compact.headline: Trusted by The Pros
ads_compact.body: Good enough for an England call up. Better in Raging Bull 💪 Explore the pieces trusted by the pros for a reason. Shop now.

ads_compact.headline: Two for £70
ads_compact.body: Two icons. One offer. Get 2 Raging Bull polos for £70 💸 Timeless heritage style, built to last and made with premium cotton. Pay later with Klarna.

ads_compact.headline: Made for the Country Man
ads_compact.body: The man who knows rural knows Raging Bull 💪 Spring layers crafted for sharp mornings, long days, and life lived properly outdoors 🌿

hero_concept.hook: The shirt lads actually asked for.
hero_concept.headline: Army Rugby, Off The Pitch
hero_concept.primary_text:
Phil kept getting the same message from Army lads: 'Any chance of a proper long-sleeve version in red?'

So we made one. Same fit that survived twenty seasons of rucks and pub crawls, now in long sleeve, deep Army red, and cut for blokes who wear their shirt on a Saturday and don't want to change before the pub.

Sizes S to 6XL. In stock now while your size holds.
hero_concept.visual_direction: The red Army Rugby Union long sleeve laid flat on weathered oak with a pint glass and a rugby ball just in frame, shot from above in warm pub light.
hero_concept.fills_gap: Right now every ad you run is either the founder story or a general 'built to last' line. None of them show a shopper a single actual shirt they can buy today. This ad names one product, tells a small human story, and gives them one thing to click.
hero_concept.image_prompt: A photorealistic editorial product photograph featuring the product from the reference image, styled in a flat-lay composition on aged oak pub table with a half-full pint glass and a worn leather rugby ball just visible at the edges of frame, with warm low pub lighting from a window at 45 degrees casting soft shadows and a golden ambient glow. Heritage British menswear aesthetic - moody, grounded, tactile, evoking a Saturday afternoon in a country pub after the match. Square 1:1 composition with breathing room top and bottom for ad text overlay. CRITICAL - preserve the exact deep Army red colorway of the rugby jersey cotton exactly as shown, AND every embroidered crest, printed graphic, sponsor logo, collar detail, and text element exactly as it appears on the reference garment - strictly NO color shift to burgundy, maroon, orange-red, or pink, NO fading, NO removing of embroidered badges or printed elements, NO altering of logo colors or placements. STRUCTURE - preserve the exact construction of the rugby shirt: long sleeves at full length, traditional rugby collar with visible button placket at the neck (preserve the exact number of buttons shown), reinforced shoulder and elbow panels if present, straight hem, regular rugby fit. PRESERVE every embroidered crest, sponsor badge, chest logo, sleeve marking, and any printed or woven brand label exactly as shown - every emblem, every text element, all colors, same placement, same scale. Render the product's metal, color, finish, fabric, weave, stones, surface treatment, AND every visible construction detail (silhouette, closures, hardware count, pockets, sleeves, neckline, printed graphics) EXACTLY as they appear in the reference image. Do NOT change the metal type, do NOT shift the color temperature, do NOT introduce tones not present in the reference, do NOT add or remove buttons, snaps, zippers, pockets, straps, or any hardware, do NOT alter the silhouette, do NOT remove or modify printed graphics or text on the garment. IMPORTANT: the 'no text' and 'no logos' items in the NEGATIVE list below apply ONLY to text or logos added as overlay to the photograph itself - text, logos, graphics, prints, and brand labels that exist ON the product in the reference image are part of the product and must be preserved exactly. Premium commerce photography quality. NEGATIVE: no overlay text added to the photograph, no overlay logos added to the photograph, no watermarks, no UI elements, no Facebook interface, no clickable buttons, no faces, no models, no collage, no borders, no duplicate products, no color shift, no metal swap, no silver-to-gold or gold-to-silver conversion, no warm-tone bias on cool metals, no recoloring of fabric or stones, no pattern modifications, no removing or replacing the product's printed graphics, no extra hardware not present in reference, no added pockets or pleats, no silhouette alterations, no design modifications.
hero_concept.reference_title: Army Rugby Union Long Sleeve Rugby - Red

next_step.urgency: Your top five ads all wear out inside the next week. Every day past that, you're paying for views most people won't see.
next_step.headline: Walk through the Raging Bull Leisure refresh plan with Kyle
next_step.body:
Simon, in this 30 min call, we'll map out:
• 3 customer avatars at your price point
• The motivational hook driving each
• A 30-day creative + spend roadmap (to outbeat your current ROAS)

Yours to keep - work with us or don't.

method_note: Based only on your public Facebook and Instagram ads - the pictures, the words, and how long each one has been running. No access to your account or private numbers.
